text: Hello and welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Sonika and today I have Abhinav from Spine.ai.
Hello Abhinav. Hi. Thank you for joining us. Thanks for having me. Abhinav, can you tell us a bit about Spine.ai?
Yeah, so at Spine we are solving global internet cataloging with computer vision at its core.
So we are using AI to help businesses shoot, edit and publish their catalogs on marketplaces on scale at a fraction of cost and time.
Image processing, right? So I assume these are like many images. Can you talk about what scale are you dealing with?
Yeah, so we process around 100k images every day.
Okay, and who are your users? What category do they belong?
So our product is used by AT plus enterprises globally in food, automobile, fashion and retail industries.
Okay, and let's say this one user uploads the images. How does this platform help?
So like users can upload images from a lot of sources like SDK, APIs, our application as well as web.
Once we get the images, they are passed on to our gateway. That's where the images come from.

And appropriately passed it to the application load valancer, where we decide that on the basis of the category of the image and how exactly it is to be processed, on which ECS cluster would this image go through.
Okay, can you tell us a bit more on the ECS cluster because you spoke about multiple categories, right?
Yeah, so every image comes with a lot of processing parameters and metadata and how exactly will the image be processed.
Okay, so once it gets to ECS, we save that parameters and metadata to our RDS as well.
And our RDS actually powers our dashboard where users can go view their catalogs as well as download them.
Okay, so once the category is done right, what do you do next?
Okay, once we have all the info about image, how exactly it will be processed, we push those images to their appropriate queues.
Okay, and once the category is decided, the image moves to the appropriate queue, how do you process those images?
Okay, now we know that what sort of model would be, would that image go through and what sort of middleware, CPU middleware, would that image go through to have that sort of output.
So then from SQS, that image is consumed by the appropriate AI service.
Okay, which means, you mean the processing layer?
Exactly.
So these instances, what are you exactly doing at this processing layer?
So we have divided the processing pipeline into three major categories.
It's pre, infer, and post.
In pre, we do stuff like resizing, color correction.
In post, we do creating images from the output that we get from base.
And in infer, we treat the images to their appropriate GPU models.
Okay, so I assume that these are like different work types, right?
Exactly.
So how do you decide what EC2 instance will you use?
Yeah, so for pre and post, we let the image go through CPU instances because those are CPU intensive workloads.
For inference, that image goes through GPU workloads.
And for GPU, we use G5 instances that are like 8n GPUs.
Okay, and you spoke about inference, right?
So I assume these are like AI models?
Exactly.
So how are you training these AI models?
So we have this training pipeline.
So once, first we upload the input data to S3, the input training data.
Once we have the input training data, we trigger SageMaker.
Once we trigger the SageMaker, it gets the input data from S3.
It gets the input data and starts the training.
We check the losses and accuracy of like how the training is going.
And once the training completes, it uploads the weights to S3.
Okay, so once the weights are there at the S3, how do you update the processing layer?
Yeah, so once we have the weights on S3, we check for it.
We have a basic benchmarking process which the weights go through.
And once it passes our benchmarks and accuracy, the SageMaker triggers the Lambda.
Okay, from where the Lambda synchronizes the weights from S3 to the processing services.
Okay, and what are you doing to make this because you are doing image processing.
So I assume that training data is a lot.
So once you have so much of training data, what exactly are you doing at SageMaker to optimize it?
So in SageMaker, we are leveraging spot instances.
So with spot instances, we have been able to reduce our cost to one third of it just using spot instances.
And not just the cost point of it, with SageMaker, we have been able to distribute the resources
and manage them efficiently and effectively within our team.
Oh, that's great.
Thank you so much, Abhinav, for sharing this architecture with us.
Thank you for watching.
This is my architecture.
Thank you.
